colon_adenocarcinoma: Distinguished from benign colon tissue by the presence of malignant glandular structures and tissue invasion.
Colon adenocarcinoma shows irregular, crowded glands, unlike the orderly appearance of benign tissue.
This class is defined by nuclear atypia and desmoplastic stroma, features absent in non-cancerous samples.
Unlike lung cancers, colon adenocarcinoma arises from the colon and has glandular differentiation.
The aggressive growth pattern and architectural distortion set it apart from all benign and lung classes.
colon_benign_tissue: Characterized by well-organized glands and uniform cells, unlike the atypia seen in adenocarcinoma.
Benign colon tissue lacks the invasive and disorganized features of malignant tumors.
This class is free of abnormal growths, nuclear pleomorphism, or tissue invasion.
Unlike lung tissues, the architecture is specific to the colon and shows no cancerous changes.
Serves as a baseline for comparison with all malignant and lung tissue classes.
lung_adenocarcinoma: Distinguished from benign lung tissue by the presence of malignant glandular or acinar structures.
Lung adenocarcinoma shows cellular atypia and invasive growth, unlike the uniformity of benign tissue.
This class is specific to the lung and features mucin production and gland formation.
Unlike squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma has glandular rather than squamous differentiation.
The irregular, crowded glands and malignant features set it apart from all benign and colon classes.
lung_benign_tissue: Characterized by healthy alveolar structures and uniform cells, unlike the atypia of lung cancers.
Benign lung tissue lacks the abnormal growth, nuclear pleomorphism, and invasion seen in carcinomas.
This class is free of tumor formation and architectural distortion.
Unlike colon tissues, the structure is specific to the lung and shows no malignant changes.
Serves as a reference for distinguishing all malignant and colon tissue classes.
lung_squamous_cell_carcinoma: Distinguished from adenocarcinoma by the presence of keratinization and intercellular bridges.
Lung squamous cell carcinoma shows polygonal tumor cells and keratin pearls, unlike glandular tumors.
This class is specific to the lung and features squamous rather than glandular differentiation.
Unlike benign lung tissue, it displays marked cellular atypia and invasive growth.
The sheets or nests of atypical squamous cells set it apart from all benign and colon classes.
